evolved_hypotheses.title: Nucleation-Controlled Ostwald Ripening with Polymer Inhibition Predicts ASD Phase Evolution Trajectories
key_improvements: Resolves nucleation dominance: specifies exactly when nucleation vs growth controls outcome
Polymer inhibition: quantifies selective inhibition of crystallization vs LLPS
Trajectory prediction: provides three distinct evolution regimes with crossover criteria
Mechanistic depth: names molecular interactions (H-bonding, steric hindrance)
Testable parameters: k_ads measurable via crystallization inhibition assays
addresses_vulnerabilities: Nucleation kinetics may dominate over growth kinetics

evolved_hypotheses.title: Pressure-Fracture Competition Regime Map for ASD Manufacturing Optimization
key_improvements: Regime boundaries: quantitative criteria (Pc number) to predict which mechanism dominates
Material properties: connects to measurable ASD properties (modulus, fracture stress)
Manufacturing relevance: specific process recommendations for different drug loadings
Scalable framework: works across particle sizes and ASD compositions
Addresses counter-evidence: explains why compression sometimes increases dissolution
addresses_vulnerabilities: Particle fracture may overwhelm pressure effects

evolved_hypotheses.title: Hybrid Buffer-Switch Model for pH-Adaptive ASD Dissolution Control
key_improvements: Embraces limitations: works with pH-dependent polymer behavior instead of against it
Dual-mode operation: combines buffering AND switching for broader applicability
Adaptive region: identifies novel autocatalytic dissolution regime
Clinical mapping: specific predictions for fed/fasted conditions
Hysteresis prediction: accounts for pH history effects
addresses_vulnerabilities: Inherent pH-dependent polymer behavior limits scope
Bulk pH effects may overwhelm microenvironment

mechanistic_clusters_maintained: Kinetic (H2.4-E): Nucleation-growth competition with polymer effects
Mechanical (H2.1-E): Stress-dependent dissolution with manufacturing optimization
Chemical (H2.3-E): pH-adaptive dissolution with dual-mode operation

examples: H2.4-E: Added nucleation rate equations and polymer inhibition constants
H2.1-E: Defined dimensionless competition number (Pc) for regime boundaries
H2.3-E: Specified pH regions and switch equations
H2.4-E: Added erosion term for polymer layer dynamics
H2.3-E: Combined buffering and switching mechanisms
